name: Iraq
experiences: Baghdad's museum core, Abbasid streets and riverfront promenades
UNESCO-listed Babylon ruins and the Lion of Babylon outside Hillah
Najaf and Karbala's major shrine precincts with deep Shia pilgrimage history
Erbil Citadel, bazaars and the easier self-planned feel of the Kurdistan Region
Optional deep-south extension to the Ziggurat of Ur and a mashhoof boat ride in the Mesopotamian Marshes
avoid: June to September is extremely hot and dusty across Baghdad and southern Iraq, so long outdoor site days become punishing.

Security conditions can change quickly; Kurdistan Region around Erbil is usually the easiest and safest independent base, while Baghdad and the south need more conservative transport choices and daylight movement.
Arbaeen, Ashura and major religious dates bring enormous pilgrimage crowds and hotel pressure in Najaf and Karbala, so book far ahead or choose quieter weeks.
stopoverNote: If this is your first Iraq trip, keep Baghdad for core museums, use pre-booked taxis for Babylon and the shrine corridor, and treat Erbil as the softest final stop because the Kurdistan Region is usually the most straightforward independent base.

cities.name: Baghdad
cities.notes: Best for the Iraq Museum, old book-market streets, river walks and practical flight connections before heading south or north.

cities.name: Erbil (Kurdistan)
cities.notes: The most relaxed independent stop in this plan, with the UNESCO citadel, bazaars, parks and easy hotel infrastructure.

cities.name: Babylon
cities.notes: Hillah-based heritage stop for Babylon's archaeological zone, the Lion of Babylon and Iraq's flagship Mesopotamian ruins.

cities.name: Najaf/Karbala
cities.notes: Twin-city pilgrimage corridor anchored by Najaf airport, major shrines, conservative dress norms and the best launchpad here for a longer south-Iraq extension.

itinerary.sim: Asiacell is the easiest one-SIM choice across Baghdad, Babylon, Najaf and Erbil, with Korek strongest in Kurdistan; plan roughly ₹630–₹1,575 for a prepaid SIM or eSIM bundle with enough data for maps, WhatsApp and airline updates.

Baghdad.name: Baghdad
Baghdad.note: Baghdad rewards an early start, hotel-arranged or trusted taxis, and enough buffer for museum security checks and Friday hour changes.

days.theme: National treasures, the old book market and the central Tigris core

activities.name: National Museum of Iraq (The Iraq Museum)
activities.tip: Recent foreigner ticket reports cluster around 25,000 IQD.

days.theme: Monuments, Abbasid-era traces and a slower riverside evening

activities.name: Baghdad Tower exterior and park zone

Babylon.name: Babylon
Babylon.note: Most travelers visit from Baghdad or continue onward to Karbala/Najaf; start early because exposed ruins get harsh in midday heat.

days.theme: UNESCO ruins, processional reconstructions and Mesopotamian iconography

activities.name: Ancient City of Babylon archaeological site
activities.tip: Recent foreigner admission reports usually quote about 25,000 IQD.

activities.name: Processional Way and reconstructed Ishtar Gate area
Saddam Hussein's Babylon Palace hilltop viewpoint

Najaf/Karbala.name: Najaf/Karbala
Najaf/Karbala.note: Dress modestly, expect heavier security near the shrines, and treat the south-Iraq extension to Ur and the Marshes as a very long guided day or an overnight add-on.

days.theme: Najaf's shrine heart, cemetery history and classic pilgrimage meals
Karbala's twin-shrine axis and the ceremonial spine between them
Long south-Iraq extension for Sumerian ruins and marshland boating

activities.name: Ziggurat of Ur
activities.tip: Recent visitor reports place entry below 5,000 IQD and it is occasionally waived.

activities.name: Mesopotamian Marshes boat ride from Chibayish
activities.tip: Typical 1–2 hour mashhoof hire runs about 20,000–40,000 IQD per boat, so sharing helps.

Erbil (Kurdistan).name: Erbil (Kurdistan)
Erbil (Kurdistan).note: Erbil is usually the easiest self-managed stop in Iraq; shared taxis from the main garages are the normal way to add Shaqlawa, Sulaymaniyah or Duhok side trips beyond this core plan.

days.theme: UNESCO citadel lanes, museum stop and the bazaar below

activities.name: Erbil Citadel outer lanes and main square
activities.tip: The UNESCO citadel itself is generally free to walk; only some interior museums or exhibits charge separately.

activities.name: Erbil Citadel Archaeological Museum
activities.tip: Recent museum entry reports usually land around 2,500–5,000 IQD.

days.theme: Mosque architecture, major city parks and a calmer Kurdish evening
Optional Kurdish soft day with cafés, shopping and easy logistics

connections.method: Pre-booked taxi or private driver via Hillah
Taxi or private car between Hillah, Karbala and Najaf
Domestic flight from Najaf (or Baghdad fallback) to Erbil, plus airport taxi

extras: Kurdistan Region is usually the calmest independent base in Iraq; if you want a gentler first stop, you can reverse this route and begin in Erbil before doing Baghdad and the south.

Between Iraqi cities outside Kurdistan, pre-arranged taxis or drivers are more practical than trying to improvise bus links; inside Kurdistan, shared taxis from formal garages are the everyday budget transport norm.

National Museum of Iraq and Babylon both commonly quote foreigner pricing that converts to about ₹1,575, while Erbil Citadel's main lanes are free and only the small museum ticket is charged separately.
For Najaf and Karbala, modest dress is essential and shrine security can be strict; carry socks, avoid shorts, and expect bag checks at each major entrance.

The Ur plus Marshes excursion is the least convenient piece of this four-city plan, so upgrade to a guided long day or overnight if those southern sites are your main priority rather than forcing them into a rushed shrine-city schedule.
